attrs.alt: Default value applies when no Provider is in the ancestor tree

content.text: The default value is reached in three scenarios: a consumer is rendered directly without any Provider in the tree (as in a unit test), a consumer is deliberately rendered outside the Provider's subtree, or the Provider is accidentally missing (often a wiring error).
Strategy A: null Default with a Guard
as the default is the most common choice when the context is always expected to have a Provider. A
default is clearly not a valid value, making it easy to detect when the Provider is absent by checking the return value of
The custom hook pattern with a
guard converts a silent failure (component renders with
data) into a loud, actionable error. The error message names the hook and the Provider, making it easy to find the missing wiring.
Strategy B: Usable Default (Works Without Provider)

Providing a usable default value allows components to render meaningfully even without a Provider. This is valuable when building reusable component libraries where a consumer should work in isolation — outside any app-specific Provider.
renders correctly in Storybook stories or component docs without any Provider wrapper. The default serves as the stand-alone configuration.
as the default creates a value that can never be accidentally returned by a Provider, making it the strictest possible check for a missing Provider.
The undefined Trap: Missing value Prop
A subtle bug: writing a Provider with an
attribute) does NOT fall back to the createContext default. React treats the omitted value prop as
, which is a valid context value. All consumers receive
and react-specific lint rules can flag Providers with no value prop. Enable lint in your editor to catch this at authoring time.
Default Value vs Provider Initial State

The default value is often confused with the initial state of the Provider. They are unrelated: the default value is a fallback for consumers outside any Provider; the initial state is set by the Provider's internal
A usable default value makes unit tests easier: components that do not need the dynamic parts of context can be rendered without a Provider wrapper.
Choosing a Default Value: Strategy Comparison
There are three strategies for choosing a default value, each with different tradeoffs:

Use the null or Symbol strategy when the context is always required (auth, theme, locale in a full app). Use the usable default when building reusable components that should work outside any specific app context.
Storybook renders components in isolation, often without any app Providers. A usable default value means stories render without needing decorator-based Provider wrappers for every story.

When testing a component that needs a non-default context value, wrap with a Provider and set the
prop directly to the test value — no need to mount the full Provider with its

Nested Providers allow overriding the context value for a subtree. The inner Provider's value is what the consumer receives; the outer Provider's value applies outside the inner one. This is unrelated to the default value — both Providers supply values.

is evaluated once when the module loads. It cannot be a function that is called at runtime to produce a fresh value — if you need a dynamic fallback, use the guard-hook pattern to throw a descriptive error instead.

The Default Value in Component Libraries

When publishing a reusable component library, providing a sensible default value means consumers of the library get working components out of the box without complex setup. The default can be derived from standard browser APIs or well-known conventions.

default guard throws due to a missing Provider, the error propagates to the nearest error boundary. Without one, the entire React tree unmounts. In production, wrap context consumers with an error boundary to localise the failure.
